speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Gustav Goldberger. Today's date is February the 27th, 1997. I'm conducting an interview with Mr. Henry Laurant. The interview is being conducted in Bethesda, Maryland, United States. It is conducted in English. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Gustav Goldberger. Today's date is February the 27th, 1997. I'm conducting an interview with Mr. Henry Laurant. The interview is being conducted in Bethesda, Maryland, United States, in English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Good afternoon. On behalf of the Shoah Foundation, I want to thank you for allowing us to do this interview. For the record, would you state your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry Warner Laurant, formerly Hans Werner Levy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you spell both of those names?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them? [LAUGHS] All right. Henry is Henry with a Y; Warner, W-A-R-N-E-R; Laurant, L-A-U-R-A-N-T. And my original name had the same initials and was instead of Henry, Hans, H-A-N-S; instead of Warner, Werner, W-E-R-N-E-R; instead of Laurant, Levy, L-E-V-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: And that makes you how old today?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: --almost 73. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what was then called Konigsberg, the capital of East-- province of East Prussia in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell Konigsberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-E-N-I-G-S-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that currently in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Immediately following the Second World War, it was annexed to the-- it was part of the [SIGHS] eastern half of East Prussia, which was annexed to Russia. That-- I'm saying Russia because it's not the USSR, not the Soviet Union it was an annexed to, but the Russian Republic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that means that even though now the USSR has disappeared as a federation, it is-- the, the area in which Konigsberg-- then Konigsberg was located still belongs as an integral part of the Russian Republic. The--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now called Kaliningrad, by the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: The family that you were born into, you had a father and a mother. What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Walter Levy and my mother was Erna Levy, nee Behrendt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Behrendt?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-H-R-E-N-D-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any siblings when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- yes, when I was born, I had a sister two years older than myself, Anneliese, A-N-N-E-L-I-E-S-E. And she was born in July of 1922, [SIGHS] so just, just short of two years before me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall back to a time when you were conscious of, of being in Konigsberg as a young child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, indeed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe that time frame?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess the earliest would be-- this was a very, very old town at that time. I say at that time because it was totally destroyed during the war and then rebuilt by the Russians. But at that time, it was a very historic town with old buildings. And I recall very well that we had an apartment which looked out over what was called the Palace Lake. There was a palace on a hill, all very Ruritanian type. And we looked out onto this lake. And the lake had boating and the-- small lake-- boating in the summer and ice skating in the winter. So it was very pleasant. We had a balcony. And I still have photographs of my sister and I on, on that particular balcony.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about how old were you when you recall this?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] At that time, I guess I must have been about four or five.

speaker: interviewer
text: You recall interacting with your sister at all at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, indeed. We played together. We-- I was the younger, so she adored me as her little brother. And of course, I adored her as my beautiful older sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the activities that you recall engaging in, either by yourself or with your sister as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [SIGHING] I can't recall too many, but typical, typical children's things. We would play mainly-- together, we would play mainly in the house and in the-- in the garden somewhat. Then, of course, we gradually got our own friends. And then we would-- as we get-- got older, we would then play somewhat more with the-- with the friends of the same sex, usually, as with-- as with each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any friends by name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't, as a matter of fact. None of my friends-- no, none of my friends. I do recall the-- I recall my sister's best friend, who started out as a school friend, and they went through several schools together. And-- I mean, they were-- they were from elementary school to, to high school, et cetera. And the reason I remember it is because it was a rather strange name for a German girl. Her nickname, at least, was Jimmy. And she had-- as a small child, apparently, she was read a story [SIGHS] about someone called Jimmy, a boy called Jimmy. And she said, I, I want to be Jimmy. And so she remained Jimmy, at least into her teens, which was the last I saw of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- our address on the lake? Only the street. The street was called Munzstrasse.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Munz?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-U-N-Z, which-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 'Munz' actually means coin. So this would be the, I suppose-- on the-- on the street, there had been-- in the area, there had been a building where you-- where you make coins. The, the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that was many, many years ago. And so that all that remained of that, of course, was the name. And the number, I don't remember. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The name of the lake, if you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schlossteich. 'Teich' is lake and 'Schloss' is palace. And so this was the lake that belonged to the Schloss.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how big of a lake-- could you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Oh, it would be smaller than the lake in Central Park, smaller than the Serpentine-- quite a bit smaller than the Serpentine in London. I'm just giving [LAUGHS] lakes that people may know. I don't know too many lakes otherwise. It was a really small city-type lake.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had benches sitting around?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were benches around it, walks around it. It was rather nicely kept with, with bushes, flowering bushes in the right seasons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember playing around the lake?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, indeed. Yes, indeed--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: --running games, catch, that kind of thing. And also on the lake, in winter, I had my first lessons in that experience of ice skating.

speaker: interviewer
text: The house that you lived in during that period of time, can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was an apartment, a fairly large apartment. And actually that one, I can't really describe. My memory doesn't go that far back because I'm not aided by any photographs that were taken inside that flat-- that apartment. I know it was fairly large. This is what people-- middle-class people in, in Europe, as you know, lived mostly in apartments, far, far less in houses than, than Americans do-- or in England, for that matter-- and especially, of course, in cities. So to find a house in a-- in a city was very rare. And these would be large apartments, typically in buildings about maybe four or five stories high, at most.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your apartment, was it high up, or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: About the second or third floor, I would think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall your father during those days? How was he occupied?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had been a doctor for quite a few years before I was born, a pediatrician. And he had his-- now the next apartment that we had, I know that he had his practice, which was quite customary in Europe at that time. He had his, his, his consulting rooms in the apartment. One part of the apartment was set aside for that. And it was separate enough so that family and patients didn't get mixed up with each other. In that [SIGHS] apartment, on the Munzstrasse when I was smaller, I cannot tell you whether he was actually in the-- whether he, he had his consulting rooms, his practice, inside the apartment, or whether he had separate consulting rooms. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the address of the second, second house-- home that you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: The second one that I recall is a much longer name. And it is Weissgerber Strasse, which is W-E-I-S-S-G-E-R-B-E-R. And a Gerber is German-- the word 'Gerber' is German for tanner, tanning leather, a tanner. Weissgerber, I have no idea. They must have tanned something white. But anyway, this, as many other street names, was called after a guild, presumably from many, many years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you interact with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] He was a very busy man. He was a very intellectual man, also highly intelligent and highly sensitive, read a lot. And I guess I inherited my love of books from him, at least I like to think so. And he was somewhat distant-- loving, yes-- but the time that we spent with him was, obviously, under the circumstances, far, far less than the time that we spent with my mother. And even though there were at least one, maybe two servants in the house-- a maid, a cook, et cetera-- in the apartment, I don't know whether they were live-in or not. I'm sure that the nanny was probably live-in. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That-- the, the division of labor between the parents in the '20s, 1920s, was-- in, in, in that kind of society was simply-- had, as a result, that the father was far less in touch with the-- with the children and that they were really the preserve of the mother and the-- and the staff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that you would see him daily, weekly? Or did he travel a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we would see him every day, for meals at the very least. I don't know at what, what age, exactly, we started sitting at the adult's table. I do recall that when we were much smaller, we had a small table set for us at the side of the big table. And we would eat somewhat earlier, but there was always an overlap before my parents took their places at the big table. And that-- that's somewhat vague. The timing is somewhat vague. And maybe I invented part of this because usually the children eat before adults do, so I can't swear to that. But anyway, the small table is definitely-- was definitely there. And this was particularly clear-- and is particularly in my memory because when people were invited, which was very often the case, we-- both of us-- both my sister and I-- dearly resented being relegated to the children's table. Oh, children's table, we can't sit-- we are not really fully fledged beings. We were relegated to the small table. Even though everybody was very nice to us, but we looked up-- literally up-- at the big table and were envious--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was in the same room?

speaker: interviewee
text: --as are all children.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was in the same room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Separate rooms.

speaker: interviewee
text: Same room-- same room--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, same room.

speaker: interviewee
text: --same room. Yes, yes, right by the side of the big table.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother? Can you describe her?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a very warm, loving person. Where my father was intellectually inclined, my mother was artistically inclined. She already did at that time quite a bit of drawing and later painting. And she then, during our last period or last years in Konigsberg, started taking sculpture lessons from a-- from a teacher there. And she did, then, until the time I parted from her, which was about, well, quite a few years later. She did a lot of that in her free time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you recall your, your early childhood as being a warm and friendly atmosphere?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The early time, I would say, would-- was, was certainly-- I do recall it as a-- as a very warm time. And I don't mean to imply that my father was not warm. He was just occupied with other things most of the time. But weekends and holidays, I recall those that were usually [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things where the full family participated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any religious practice in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I cannot recall any. As a small child-- that's when we're talking about, that period-- I cannot recall any at all. That came-- started to come at the time that we moved into the other apartment with the long name. And that was the last apartment that we had before we left Konigsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Konigsberg, did you ever visit a synagogue as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did, as a matter of fact. When, when I, I took your question to mean was the family involved as such. And my answer went to that. I was involved in-- I was probably the most involved in Jewish life, Jewishness, for two reasons. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were-- we left Konigsberg when I was 12. And it may seem strange, but even at the age of-- let's see, I would be eight when I started, eight or nine when I started going to the high school there, the gymnasium. And at-- even before that time, I was very, very aware of what it was like to be a Jew. The reason being, of course, the usual kind of discrimination at school, I say usual-- usual for Germany. Not-- but it was quite usual there, as I soon was told. There wasn't any physical abuse, a lot verbal abuse, more or less forceful, more or less insulting, but always there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I should add that religion in Europe was at that time-- and still is to a greater extent than in Anglo-Saxon countries-- a-- much more of a part of one's identity. On every identity document that I know of, whether it was a-- an identity card for use within the country, on a passport, whatever it might be, on the first page, among the vital statistics would be religion. And that would be marked. In the same way, this was part of the identity that accompanies you everywhere-- accompanied you everywhere-- school, work, et cetera. You would-- it was not, not possible even to, to, to circumvent that or to, to escape it. And if one belonged to a despised or disliked-- at least at first, disliked, later, despised and worst-- minority, i.e. the Jews, it was, of course, the, the, the, the, words, the foul language, the words that fell on the ears of children already from a very early age. My earliest-- I can't tell exactly, but I'm sure that it was something like-- it was during my first year at the elementary school, which starts at five at that time. Or five is a kind of a kindergarten class. So kindergarten was five. It was during that year that I heard-- on my way home from school, I was walking-- wasn't that far-- I heard some boys talking loudly behind me. And I heard some words repeated again and again. And then the equivalent of, hey, you, since I didn't pay any attention.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [LAUGHS] I really, literally, probably didn't know what, what they were-- what they were doing, what they were aiming at. And so, so I said, yes, very attentively, you know, yes, do you want something? And so they started spewing these, these words at me, et cetera. And I was absolutely horrified.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- no, I, I had really no idea. I thought they'd got the wrong-- the wrong child. They're making a mistake.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I just sort of beat a hasty retreat because I thought they were slightly deranged, actually. So that was my first experience of-- practical experience of anti-Semitism. And I wish all others had been as, as peaceful as that one. So-- [SIGHS] Do you recall the words, the actual German words, that were spoken by those children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [SIGHS] it would be-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I don't really. It would be all kinds of what they imagined to be Yiddish versions of German curses. It would be all kinds of Jewish names. 'Itzik' would be one, which was presumably their way that they thought 'Yitzhak' was-- was pronounced. And so this was supposed to be an insult, simply-- merely by dint of being a Jewish name. This gives you some idea of the culture within these-- within which these children were brought up, that they simply shouted, as if it were an insult, in the tone in which an insult is shouted, a Jewish name, which is a perfectly fine name. And it is meant as and taken as-- taken as because of the tone-- as an insult. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many--

speaker: interviewee
text: But there are many worse ones as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jewish classmates do you recall? I mean, were you one of very few or one of many Jews in that particular school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that particular school, I don't remember any. I didn't make that strong-- I was still pretty hazy, at four years old, as to what it was. I-- when I got home, I-- I asked my parents, presumably-- I mean, I, I can't tell you that I remember specifically doing this. But I was a pretty curious and a bright child, so I'm quite sure I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: What were those boys about? And so that's where I got my first little education in what kind of place we were living in. But it was all mild, adapted to a child's tender sensibilities. And so it was not really very exact.

speaker: interviewee
text: But gradually, I began to see. Once I had that grounding, I began to see all kinds of nuances as I went about my, my life at school and, and outside of school. And I began to sense and see and feel and hear the, the, the discrimination very, very strongly. And the, the discrimination was always very insulting, very negative.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did it get increasingly worse for you as you proceeded on with the grades in elementary school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In elementary school, I assume it did. I can remember elementary school only very little. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I may remember it little because I really don't remember all. I remember it little because I repressed it as maybe a too unpleasant memory to, to, to carry with me. When I then started high school-- which was early, before the-- before high school is usually started-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was about age eight, nine, I, I suppose, must have been. And there, I remember it much more clearly because there, it was on an adult note there. The, the, the, the last year at elementary school was the final year of childhood, in that sense. First year of high school was the first year of adolescence, the first experience of adolescence. Even though I was only nine, I was surrounded by boys that ranged typically from 10 to 18. And so-- and I was treated far more as an adult, insulted more as an adult. There was quite a dividing line between the tone in elementary school and at high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you also in high school sort of alone as a Jew or did you have associates?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the high school, I had two friends. One was a full Jew. The other was a half-Jew. And the-- they were both friends, no more. They weren't close friends. The chap whose-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whom I call a half-Jew, i.e., his, his father-- his name was Blum, B-L-U-M. I think his name was Gerdt. But anyway, he was a provincial ice-skating champion. He was a very good athlete, and-- already starting as a child. And he could have, if he wanted-- he was kind of wooed by the other boys and-- in the sense that he could have declared himself to be with them and jeered at the Jews, or he could have done what he did, which was to declare-- to get beaten up rather than do the beating up, which-- and we were side by side. We were both-- I was not an athletic champion. But, but I was more physical. Poor [? Sigmund, ?] who was the third, was really a very-- a very physically weak boy. And, and he-- so weak that, that he wasn't even good sport for them to, to, to fight with and to beat up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for that we-- Blum and myself-- we sort of stood shoulder to shoulder. [LAUGHS] And we had to explain to our parents why we were bloodied when we got home-- very often, not every day, but quite often. You say it didn't happen every day. But was it quite often, as you say, that these incidents--

speaker: interviewee
text: Fairly often, yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: --took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: --yes. It was something to be-- not to look forward to, shall we say. [SIGHS] And, of course, the worst part is you can say these are children, mostly members of Hitler Youth. Many of them were in their Hitler Youth uniforms, came to school that way. And they-- you might say they're still children, nonetheless. The-- maybe the worst part about this was the behavior of the masters, the teachers, who had the supervision during the breaks. And, of course, it was during the breaks that this happened. And they just turned their backs. They just turned their backs, engaged in very animated conversation, one with the other, anything, so that they wouldn't have to look that way, where we were getting ours and giving some back, I must say.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't recall any favorable intervention by the teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only one, and that was very ironic, quite, quite ironic because it was-- I was running. And I was not running from anybody. I was simply running. I was actually, for a change, playing a running game, I suppose, with somebody. I stumbled, and I fell. And I fell with such force that it knocked the breath out of me completely. And I guess I must have fainted for, for a little while. And the next, next thing I remember was that, that some boys-- they, they were not Jewish boys-- some of my fellow students and, and torturers were carrying me up, up the stairs of the building, of the school building. And at the top there was a-- there was a-- there was a teacher standing. And he-- he was quite kind. Because they were saying to their-- to the-- I'm sorry. Not the two that were carrying me up, but others were saying, oh, you've got the Jew there. That's good. You beat him unconscious, now-- that kind of thing. And they didn't know what to say. They couldn't say, no, no, we're just-- we're helping him because that would give them bad standing [LAUGHS] with their-- with their Nazi comrades. And so all the teachers saw was that I was unconscious. I was being helped by two boys. And, and he was, under the circumstances, he thought maybe I had been knocked unconscious and that would have been going too far at that time. That was still going too far. And so that was, I would say, the only time during school, during, during classes. There was a lot of anti-Semitic matter inserted into the lessons, especially for history, geography, literature, in which-- the, the, the usual way, what you would expect. If you're talking music, Felix Mendelssohn was mentioned as an inferior imitator of the great German composers, the usual kind of thing. But I imagine that in Russia it would be mentioned as-- [LAUGHS] they would be mentioned as, as imitators of the great Russian composers. So-- but in all kinds of ways, you were made to feel separate and that you were not judged by the same standards. Your marks tended to be lower. You had to work twice as hard to even get the same marks, never mind better marks, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did there come a time when you just felt that you've had enough of that and you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how did you deal with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: --very very early. And the only way in which I could express this was by joining a Zionist organization, which I found out existed. And this was the Habonim. And I was a member of the Habonim. And my father was, was in favor, which was very, very generous of him because his own convictions were quite the other way. He was not a Zionist. And in, in Germany, as in other places, there were two large categories of Jews, one assimilated and one Zionist. In Germany, the division was particularly strong, particularly direct, because the Jews were, by and large, the professional classes, the business classes, and they were pretty well off.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape number 1.
